head.ce:title: Carbon dots-grafted LaSrAl3O7:Sm3+ nanocomposites for AI-assisted twin latent fingerprints recognition, poroscopic studies and high-performance lighting applications

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: FP analysis is a reliable personal identification method due to the uniqueness and permanence of fingerprint patterns. Dactyloscopy, the study of these patterns, is vital in forensic science for matching crime scene prints to individuals. Each finger has distinct ridge patterns, with no two fingerprints identical even among identical twins. Fingerprints are classified as patent (visible), plastic (impressed), or latent (invisible). LFPs, formed from skin secretions, are the most common yet hardest to detect, influenced by surface type, environment, and skin condition. Successful identification depends on effectively visualizing and preserving these prints. Advances in technology have improved evidence collection and analysis. Despite biometric alternatives like palm prints and facial recognition, fingerprints remain crucial in forensics. LFP technology combines chemistry, biology, and technology to enhance ridge patterns for accurate identification, linking suspects to crime scenes and aiding investigations. Forensic databases like the FBI’s IAFIS enable rapid identification, supporting justice and security [1,2].

Fingerprint matching in twins highlights genetic influence on fingerprint traits. Studies show strong similarities in patterns whorls, loops, arches and features like ridge count and depth in identical twins, who share nearly identical DNA [7]. A Wall Street Journal article [3] notes their prints may be 95 % similar, possibly fooling biometric systems. However, small microdetails formed by subtle womb environment differences provide key forensic markers. Traditional LFP detection methods iodine fumigation, ninhydrin spraying, powder dusting has drawbacks: iodine requires post-treatment, ninhydrin is toxic and powders like Fe₂O₃ and TiO₂ lack sensitivity. High-contrast phosphors have emerged to better interact with LFP residues (water, salts, organics). Recent advances use CDs based nanophosphors that enhance rare earth ion fluorescence, yielding brighter, clearer fingerprint images under UV 365 nm and sharper ridge details across surfaces. While visualization research is extensive, analysis and comparison remain less explored [4]. With biometric systems growing, faster, integrated identification methods are needed to replace subjective manual processes. Computer-based recognition offers rapid, accurate comparison via high-resolution imaging and databases. However, no system yet fully integrates LFP detection and analysis, making the development of novel materials and unified approaches essential for advancing forensic fingerprint applications [5].

Phosphors have driven progress in lighting and displays. Phosphor-converted white LEDs (pc-wLEDs) are favoured for low energy use, long lifespan, brightness, and eco-friendliness. Commercial w-LEDs typically combine blue LEDs with yellow Y₃Al₅O₁₂:Ce³⁺ phosphors to create white light but often lack red, resulting in a low color rendering index (Ra) that limits light quality. Prolonged exposure to their blue light can damage retinal cells, disrupt melatonin, and affect sleep, known as blue light hazard. Using near-UV LEDs with blue, green, and red phosphors improves Ra and lowers correlated color temperature (CCT), reducing blue light risks and fitting specialized environments like operating rooms and cinemas [6]. However, the limited availability of high-quality phosphors excitable by near-UV LEDs, particularly red phosphors, hampers development.

Commercial red phosphors like Y₂O₂S:Eu³⁺ and CaS:Eu²⁺ suffer from poor chemical and thermal stability and may release toxic gases such as hydrogen sulfide. Nitride phosphors such as Sr₂Si₅N₈:Eu²⁺ and CaAlSiN₃:Eu²⁺ offer better thermal stability and high quantum efficiency but are costly to produce due to extreme synthesis conditions (1500–2000 °C at 1.0 MPa N₂) [7]. Thus, there is a strong need for red phosphors combining high efficiency, color purity, and thermal stability. Rare earth ions, especially Sm³⁺, are effective red emitters with strong n-UV absorption and red/orange-red emission via 4f–4f transitions [8]. Additionally, partial cation substitution in the host matrix can alter the crystal field and enhance phosphor luminescence.

CDs are attractive fluorescent materials due to their low cost, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and unique photoluminescence. They compete with traditional rare-earth phosphors and other materials, but face challenges like low solid-state quantum yield, aggregation quenching, limited emission tuning, and poor stability. Embedding CDs in nanophosphors improves efficiency, reduces quenching, and enhances spectral tuning for better white light [9,10]. Nanophosphors also increase thermal and electrical stability and color rendering, making them suitable for w-LEDs. However, research on solid CD-based materials is still developing, requiring further optimization for practical use.

Since Matsuzawa et al.’s pioneering work on the afterglow phosphor SrAl2O4:Eu2+, Dy3+ [11], aluminate materials have attracted attention for persistent luminescence due to their low cost and excellent chemical and physical stability. LaSrAl3O7 (LSAO), part of the RE strontium aluminate family with a melilite-like layered structure, offers diverse doping possibilities as its A and B sites accommodate various cations, enabling unique luminescent properties when doped with rare earth (RE) ions. Despite its potential, few studies have explored LSAO doped solely with Sm3+ Table 1 [12–17]. Lanthanide (Ln3+) doped phosphors are widely studied for their broad emission range from UV to infrared, featuring sharp emission lines, long lifetimes, high quantum yields, and good solubility due to their 4f electronic configurations. Different Ln3+ ions emit distinct colors for instance, Tb3+ produces sharp green emissions, and Eu3+ shows red emissions [18]. Sm3+ is notable for its strong red emission from the 4G5/2 → 6H7/2 transition, emitting in visible and near-infrared regions. Its luminescence makes it useful in OLEDs, lasers, agricultural films, solar concentrators, anticounterfeiting, and other applications [19].

CDs, known for their excellent photostability, tunable fluorescence, and surface functionalization capabilities, have emerged as versatile materials for enhancing photoluminescent properties in various host matrices. On the other hand, LSAO doped with Sm3+ is a promising orange-red emitting nanophosphor with good crystallinity, chemical stability, and efficient optical properties. While both materials exhibit desirable optical characteristics independently, their combination offers a synergistic approach to improving emission efficiency and expanding functional applications. Therefore, to further enhance the photoluminescence properties and surface activity, CDs were successfully incorporated into the LSAO:Sm3+ matrix, forming a composite system (CDs@LSAO:Sm3+). The integration of CDs not only facilitates energy transfer to Sm3+ ions, thereby enhancing emission intensity, but also improves the dispersion and processability of the nanophosphor. To assess the performance of the synthesized CDs@LSAO:Sm3+ composite in a broader context, a comparative analysis with other recently reported carbon dot-grafted nanophosphors has been included in the Table 2 [20–27]. The table provides a side-by-side comparison of key parameters such as carbon source, excitation and emission wavelength, PL enhancement, and potential applications, demonstrating that the present material exhibits competitive or superior performance relative to existing systems.

This work reports the first successful doping of Sm³⁺ ions into the LSAO matrix combined with carbon dots (CDs) to create bright orange-red emitting CDs@LSAO:Sm³⁺ NCs using a simple ultrasonication method. The CDs were green-synthesized from onion and lemon juice extracts via a one-pot microwave process. The NCs’ crystal structure, morphology, and luminescence were thoroughly studied. Under 403 nm excitation, LSAO:Sm³⁺ emits a broad peak at 598 nm, compatible with commercial n-UV (365 nm) LEDs. Incorporating CDs boosts PL intensity by 82.39 times, due to FRET between CDs and Sm³⁺ ions. The CDs@LSAO:5Sm³⁺ NCs also show high activation energy (0.31 eV), making them suitable for optical thermometry. Using these NCs, pc-wLEDs and orange-red LEDs were fabricated, demonstrating their potential in advanced lighting. This study introduces a novel synthesis route for n-UV excitable w-LED phosphors and a strategy to enhance luminescence in rare-earth-doped materials while minimizing CD-related drawbacks. Additionally, the phosphors were applied in an innovative LFP detection method, improving contrast and detail visibility under specific lighting. Automated fingerprint recognition and twin fingerprint comparison were implemented using MATLAB, reducing subjectivity and inefficiency in traditional analysis. This integrated detection, recognition, and comparison approach offers a promising path for future forensic applications.

All reagents used were of analytical grade, and every solution was prepared using freshly distilled, deionized water. Fluorescent CDs were derived from onions and lemons obtained from a local market in Tumkur, India. The chemicals required for synthesizing the nanomaterials were procured from Sigma-Aldrich, India, and included lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate [La(NO3)3·6H2O], strontium nitrate [Sr(NO3)2], aluminum nitrate nonahydrate [Al(NO3)3·9H2O], samarium nitrate hexahydrate [Sm(NO3)3·6H2O], polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), and ethanol. Additional high-purity reagents, such as hydrochloric acid (HCl) and ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH), were also employed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of CDs using onion and lemon
ce:section.ce:para: A simple, rapid, and one-step microwave-assisted carbonization technique was implemented to produce fluorescent CDs (Scheme 1 a). In a typical procedure, 8 mL of deionized water was mixed with 10 mL of a 25 % (v/v) ammonium hydroxide solution and 20 mL of filtered lemon and onion juice. This mixture was transferred into a 250 mL Erlenmeyer flask and heated in a microwave oven operating at 1450 W for 6 min. The resulting brownish-black solid was allowed to cool to room temperature, then redissolved in 100 mL of deionized water. After centrifuging the dispersion for 30 min at 6000 rpm, the supernatant was dialyzed against deionized water for 24 h using dialysis tubing (MWCO = 1 kDa) to remove any residual impurities. Finally, the CDs were recovered as a powder via evaporation, re-dispersed in deionized water and stored at 4 °C for subsequent use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthesis of pure LSAO and LSAO:xSm3+ (x = 0.5–9 mol%) NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The LSAO:Sm3+ NPs were synthesized using an ultrasonication-assisted method, employing aloe vera extract as a green fuel as well as a natural chelating and stabilizing agent. Fresh aloe vera gel was blended with 200 mL of deionized water, heated at 80 °C for 30 min, then filtered for immediate use. For the precursor solution, stoichiometric amounts of La(NO3)3·6H2O, Sr(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, and Sm(NO3)3·6H2O were dissolved in 100 mL of the aloe vera extract with continuous stirring at 70 °C for 30 min on a hot plate. This solution was then subjected to ultrasonication at 40–50 kHz and 200 W for 60 min at 60 °C, ensuring uniform chelation and nucleation, which resulted in a gel-like formation. The gel was dried at 120 °C for 12 h to remove moisture, ground into a fine powder and finally calcined at 1000 °C for 4 h to yield crystalline LSAO:Sm3+ NPs. Scheme 1b provides a schematic overview of the synthesis process.

To prepare the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs, an accurately weighed amount of LSAO:5Sm3+ powder was mixed with a predetermined quantity of CDs solution to achieve the desired weight percentages. In a glass beaker, the appropriate volumes of CDs solution and LSAO:5Sm3+ powder were combined, and deionized water was added as required to adjust consistency. This mixture was then sonicated using a probe sonicator (20–40 kHz, 100–200 W) for 30 min to ensure that the CDs were uniformly distributed on the surface of LSAO:5Sm3+. The suspension was subsequently dried in a hot-air oven at 60–80 °C until complete evaporation of the solvent, which typically required about 24 h. To further enhance the interaction between the CDs and the LSAO:5Sm3+ particles, the resulting dry composite was annealed in a furnace at 200–300 °C for 3 h and then ground into a fine powder. This procedure was repeated for CDs loadings of 2, 4, 6, and 8 wt %, with the volume of the CDs solution adjusted accordingly. A schematic illustration of this synthesis route is shown in Scheme 1c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monochromatic orange-red LED and w-LED fabrication
ce:section.ce:para: For fabricating the monochromatic orange-red LED, the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs were coated onto a 395 nm UV chip, which served as the excitation source. The NCs were dispersed in a transparent silicone resin and the phosphor-resin mixture was uniformly applied to the n-UV chip. The assembly was then cured to form a stable luminescent layer, enabling efficient PL conversion. In the case of the wLED, a 395 nm n-UV chip was used as the excitation source. An optimized blend of bright orange-red CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs, green phosphors ((Ba,Sr)2SiO4:Eu2+) and blue phosphors (BaMgAl10O17:Eu2+) in an 8:1:3 wt ratio was prepared. After thorough mixing to ensure homogeneity, the phosphor blend was incorporated into a clear silicone resin, which was then evenly coated onto the n-UV chip. This homogeneous layer efficiently converted n-UV light into visible light through PL.

To evaluate the applicability of the synthesized CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs for LFP detection, FPs were collected from a 25-year-old female donor. Both the right and left thumbprints of the donor were obtained for poroscopic analysis. Additionally, FPs from five individuals (two males and three females, aged 25, 29, 24, 27, and 30 years) were collected for comparative analysis. For twin FP studies, FPs were gathered from male twins (25 years), female twins (25 years) and male-female twins (20 years). Prior to collection, participants washed their hands with soap and water and allowed them to air dry. Subsequently, the participants lightly touched their foreheads to transfer sebum onto their fingers before pressing them onto pre-treated substrates to form initial FP impressions. The optimized CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs was then uniformly applied to an LFP dusting brush and carefully dusted over the FP substrates. Excess powder was gently removed using a fine-bristled brush to reveal clear FP patterns. Scheme 1d provides a schematic representation of the LFP detection process. The developed LFPs were visualized under UV illumination and images capturing level-1 (such as right loops and deltas), level-2 (including hooks, islands, enclosures, dots, crossovers, ridge endings, bifurcations, and double bifurcations) and level-3 (sweat pores and scars) features were recorded using a Nikon D3100/AF-S digital camera. Finally, images of FPs from twins were processed using MATLAB to calculate similarity scores between twin FP patterns.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out using a Shimadzu-7000 diffractometer equipped with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) to identify the phase and crystal structure of the materials. The surface morphology was examined using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM), Hitachi SU-8010, while the particle size was further investigated using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) from JEOL, Japan. Energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDAX), coupled with the FESEM (Hitachi SU-8010), was employed to perform elemental analysis of the samples. The chemical composition, charge states, and binding energies were studied using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), conducted on a K-Alpha electron spectrometer from Thermo Scientific with a 1 eV step size and an electron detection range of 0–1200 eV. For optical studies, photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were recorded using a Horiba spectrophotometer equipped with a xenon light source emitting at 430 nm. The internal quantum efficiency (IQE) was measured using an FLS 980 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK), fitted with a 450 W xenon lamp, an integrating sphere coated with BaSO4 and a spectral resolution of 0.05 nm. Finally, the electroluminescence (EL) properties of the fabricated white-light-emitting diode (w-LED) device were evaluated using a USB4000 optical fiber spectrometer from Ocean Optics.

Structural characterization is pivotal because it unveils key details about a material's phase, crystallinity, and overall crystal structure. In this work, XRD patterns for pure LSAO and LSAO:xSm3+ (x = 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 mol %) phosphors were recorded over an angular range of 10–80°. As depicted in Fig. 1 a, the diffraction patterns are in excellent agreement with the standard reference (PDF#25–0746) [34]. A closer look at the 2θ range of 30.2–31.0° (Fig. 1b) reveals a noticeable shift toward higher angles, which is indicative of modifications in lattice strain [28]. This shift arises from the substitution of larger La3+ ions (r = 1.032 Å) with smaller Sm3+ ions (r = 0.958 Å), causing a reduction in interplanar spacing and an increase in the diffraction angle (θ). The percentage ionic difference (Dr ) was determined using the formula [29]:

In this equation, Rs and Rd ​denote the ionic radii of the substituted and dopant ions, respectively, while CN represents the coordination number. For La3+ (CN = 6) versus Sm3+ (CN = 6), the calculated Dr is 7.17 %, which is significantly below the critical limit of 30 %, thereby facilitating effective substitution. Additionally, the XRD pattern for the synthesized CDs (Fig. 1e) exhibits a prominent peak at 2θ = 21.83°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, and a smaller peak at 2θ = 43.14°, corresponding to the (100) plane [30]. The composite material, CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+, displays characteristic diffraction peaks from both the LSAO matrix and the CDs.

Fig. 1c shows the XRD pattern of LSAO:5Sm3+ nanoparticles refined using the Rietveld method via Fullprof software. The diffraction pattern can be indexed to a tetragonal structure with space group P
4 ¯
21m (no. 113), and the unit cell dimensions are a = b = 7.923 Å and c = 5.343 Å, with α = β = γ = 90°. Refined XRD patterns for undoped LSAO and Sm3+ doped LSAO (0.5, 1, 3, 7, and 9 mol %) are presented in Fig. S1(a–f), and the refinement details are compiled in Table 3 . The LSAO host belongs to the melilite structural family. As illustrated in Fig. 1d, its three-dimensional polyhedral framework is composed of corner-sharing
A l

5 −
tetrahedra, with Sr3+ and La3+ ions randomly distributed within the channels, each coordinated by eight O2- ions in a centrosymmetric environment.

The average crystallite size of each sample was calculated using the Debye-Scherrer equation [31]:

Here, D is the average crystallite size, λ is the X-ray wavelength (1.54056 Å), k is the shape factor (≈0.9), θ is the Bragg angle, and β represents the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the diffraction peak (in radians). The calculated crystallite sizes range from 37.19 to 37.95 nm (see Table 4 ), confirming the nanocrystalline nature of the samples. The lattice strain, introduced by crystal distortions and defects, was evaluated using [32]:
(3)

Diffraction peak broadening is influenced by both crystallite size and lattice strain. While the Scherrer formula adequately describes size-induced broadening, it fails to capture the microstructural strain arising from grain boundaries, point defects, triple junctions, sinter stress, coherency stress, stacking faults, and similar factors. To address this, several methods have been developed. Notably, the Williamson-Hall (W-H) model considers both size broadening (βsize) and strain broadening (βstrain​), combining them to describe the total peak broadening.

An alternative approach, the Size-Strain Plot (SSP) method, is particularly advantageous for evaluating crystal size and strain under the assumption of isotropic line broadening [33]. The SSP method tends to yield more precise results than the W-H method because it focuses on lower-angle reflections, where data quality is superior and peak overlapping is minimal. In the SSP method, it is assumed that the size contribution follows a Lorentzian distribution while the strain contribution is Gaussian. Hence, the total broadening is expressed as:

It is important to note that an ideal XRD peak profile would be purely Lorentzian or Gaussian, however, real-world data often do not conform strictly to either function. To overcome this limitation, the symmetric Voigt function is used to model XRD peak broadening more accurately. The Halder and Wagner (H-W) model, which incorporates the Voigt function, addresses these challenges [35]. According to the H-W model, the FWHM of the physical profile is given by:

The summarized results in Table 4 underscore the exceptional crystallinity and minimal defect levels in LSAO:5Sm3+ NPs, thereby rendering them highly suitable for advanced optical applications.

The morphologies of the prepared CDs, LSAO:5Sm3+ NPs, and the composite CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ samples were examined using both FE-SEM and TEM, as illustrated in Fig. 2 . The FE-SEM images offer a general view of the surface features and structural characteristics of the synthesized materials. In Fig. 2a, the CDs are observed as uniformly distributed nanospheres with smooth surfaces a morphology that is instrumental in enhancing their fluorescence properties and ensuring even dispersion in various matrices. In contrast, Fig. 2b reveals that the LSAO:5Sm3+ NPs possess an irregular, agglomerated-flaky morphology with a rough texture. Fig. 2c shows the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs, in which the CDs are successfully embedded within the LSAO:5Sm3+ matrix. The yellow dashed circles in the image pinpoint the CDs, suggesting a robust interaction between the CDs and the host material an interaction that likely influences the optical and electronic behaviour of the NCs.

EDAX was further used to elucidate the elemental composition of both the LSAO:5Sm3+ NPs and the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs. The EDAX spectra and associated elemental maps are provided in Fig. S2. The EDAX spectrum of LSAO:5Sm3+ nanoparticles (Fig. S2a) confirms the presence of oxygen, aluminum, strontium, lanthanum, and samarium. The inset table provides the corresponding weight and atomic percentages of each element. The spectrum for CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs (Fig. S2b) exhibits a prominent carbon signal, with distinct peaks indicating the presence of carbon-containing species within the LSAO:5Sm3+ matrix. This confirms the successful incorporation of CDs into the host structure. Elemental maps for lanthanum (green), strontium (red), aluminum (blue), oxygen (parrot green), samarium (purple), and carbon (pale purple) are displayed in Fig. S2(c–g) for the LSAO:5Sm3+ NPs and in Fig. S2(h–m) for the composite CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs. These mappings illustrate a uniform elemental distribution throughout the matrices, and the statistical bar graph in Fig. S2n summarizes the variations in atomic percentages between the undoped and doped samples, thereby emphasizing the successful synthesis and compositional homogeneity of the materials.

TEM images provide further insight into the internal morphology and dispersion of the nanomaterials. In Fig. 2d, well-defined and uniformly dispersed spherical CDs are clearly visible, confirming their nanoscale dimensions and uniform carbon distribution as suggested by the image contrast. Fig. 2e, however, shows that the LSAO:5Sm3+ NPs tend to form highly agglomerated, flaky clusters with irregular shapes a behavior likely related to the synthesis process and the inherent surface energy of these particles. In Fig. 2f, the TEM image of the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs clearly demonstrates the presence of CDs as small, well-distributed particles within the LSAO:5Sm3+ matrix. The yellow dashed circles again highlight the locations of the CDs, indicating a successful integration between the carbon dots and the host, a critical factor for potential applications in optoelectronic and photonic devices. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images further confirm the crystallinity and structural integration within the composite. The HR-TEM image of the CDs (Fig. 2g) emphasizes their nanoscale nature and crystalline quality, the observed interplanar spacing of 0.325 nm corresponds to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon a characteristic signature of CDs [38]. This spacing was validated through Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) and Inverse FFT (IFFT) analyses, as shown in Fig. 2j, and the pixel profile generated with GATAN digital micrograph software [39] confirms this spacing (Fig. 2n). For the LSAO:5Sm3+ NPs, the HR-TEM image (Fig. 2h) exhibits distinct lattice fringes that are characteristic of highly crystalline domains. The measured interplanar spacing of 0.297 nm corresponds to the (211) plane of the LSAO:5Sm3+ crystal structure, as further corroborated by FFT and IFFT analyses in Fig. 2k, with the pixel profile in Fig. 2o confirming a consistent d-spacing of 0.297 nm. Finally, the HR-TEM image of the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs (Fig. 2i) clearly shows the successful embedding of CDs within the LSAO:5Sm3+ matrix. Here, distinct lattice fringes corresponding to 0.325 nm for the CDs and 0.297 nm for the LSAO:5Sm3+ are evident (Fig. 2l & 2m), and the corresponding pixel profile plots in Fig. 2p & q further verify the effective incorporation of CDs into the composite.

The FT-IR spectra for LSAO:5Sm3+, CDs and the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs are presented in Fig. S3a. In the spectrum for LSAO:5Sm3+, intense peaks observed in the 425–1040 cm-1 range are attributed to various M–O stretching and bending vibrations associated with the
A l
O 4
5 −
groups inherent in the melilite structure [40]. Additional peaks at 3641.02, 3285.95, and 1943.70 cm-1 are observed, corresponding to O–H stretching and H–O–H bending vibrations, these features are indicative of water or atmospheric moisture adsorbed on the sample surface. The FT-IR spectrum of the CDs exhibits several distinct peaks: the broad peak at 3428.37 cm-1 corresponds to O–H stretching vibrations, a peak at 2875.26 cm-1 is assigned to C–H vibrations, 1619.84 cm-1 is associated with the C=O stretching of carboxylic groups (COOH), 1454.01 cm-1 corresponds to C=C bending, 1145.76 cm-1 indicates C–N bending, and the peak at 846.29 cm-1 is attributed to asymmetric C–O vibrations [41]. These features confirm that the CDs are functionalized with oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups. Upon incorporating the CDs into the LSAO:5Sm3+ matrix to form the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs, the FT-IR spectrum displays new peaks and slight shifts. The peak at 3427.85 cm-1 is due to O–H stretching, while the peaks at 1617.23 cm-1 and 1453.48 cm-1 are indicative of C=O and C=C bending vibrations, respectively, suggesting that these functional groups are now present on the surface of the LSAO:5Sm3+. Additionally, a peak at 845.39 cm-1 corresponds to C–O vibrations, further supporting the integration of CDs into the host matrix.

Fig. S3(b–h) displays the diffuse reflectance (DR) spectra for undoped LSAO and Sm3+-doped LSAO:xSm3+ (x = 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 mol %) samples, measured over a wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Notably, the observed declines in reflectance at wavelengths below 500 nm indicate significant absorption in the near-ultraviolet region. From these DR measurements, the optical band-gap (Egap) of the materials can be determined using the relation [42]:

Here, R∞ represents the diffuse reflectance of the sample relative to a reference. By extrapolating the tangent to the x-axis from the DR plots (as shown in the inset of Fig. S3(b–h)), the band-gap values for the undoped LSAO and the various Sm3+-doped LSAO samples were determined to be 4.502, 4.458, 4.426, 4.402, 4.393, 4.340, and 4.287 eV, respectively. The slight reduction in the band-gap with increasing Sm3+ content can be attributed to the incorporation of Sm3+ ions into the LSAO matrix [44]. These DR outcomes underscore the potential application of the synthesized nanopowders in high-performance semiconductor devices, thanks to their broad optical band-gap values.

To ascertain the oxidation states and chemical composition of elements in both LSAO:5Sm3+ and the composite CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+, XPS measurements were conducted. The survey scan (Fig. S4a) was first used to locate all significant peaks. In Fig. S4b, the La 3d core level spectrum for both samples is presented. For the LSAO:5Sm3+ sample, the La 3d spectra exhibit clear multiplet splitting in the La 3d5/2 and La 3d3/2 regions, with each of these peaks deconvoluted into two components. The peaks at 834.71 eV and 851.41 eV are attributed to the spin-orbit components of La 3d5/2 and La 3d3/2, respectively, in agreement with previous literature [45]. Satellite peaks observed at 838.43 eV and 855.08 eV further confirm the La 3d multiplet splitting, indicating the presence of La3+. Fig. S4c shows the high-resolution Sr 3d spectrum, where the peaks at 132.24 eV and 134.03 eV for Sr 3d5/2 and Sr 3d3/2, respectively, verify the expected valence state. In Fig. S4d, the Al 2p spectrum is deconvoluted to a single peak at 73.82 eV. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S4e) features peaks at 530.59 eV and 531.35 eV, corresponding to La–O and Al–O bonds, respectively [46], while a lower binding energy peak at 529.36 eV is ascribed to O2- ions in oxygen-deficient regions of the LSAO matrix. Fig. S4f presents the Sm-doped LSAO spectrum, where the dominant peak at ∼1082.27 eV is attributed to Sm 3d5/2 and the one at ∼1107.20 eV to Sm 3d3/2. Literature indicates that divalent Sm (4f6) components appear between 1070.0 and 1080.0 eV, while trivalent Sm (4f5) components are observed between 1080.0 and 1090.0 eV [47]. Thus, the binding energies confirm that Sm is predominantly trivalent. In addition, the C 1 s spectrum shows a characteristic peak at 284.77 eV, typically due to adventitious carbon (in forms such as C–C, C–H, and C–O) from ambient exposure. In the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ composite, an extra C 1 s peak at 289.03 eV is observed and is attributed to the C=O bonds in the carbonate groups of the CDs (Fig. S4g). The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. S4h) displays a peak at 399.73 eV, assigned to amino nitrogen (N–H), which appears exclusively in the CDs-containing composite. Notably, slight shifts in the binding energies of the La 3d, Sr 3d, Al 2p, O 1 s, Sm 3d, and C 1 s peaks in the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ sample relative to the pure LSAO:5Sm3+ indicate subtle changes in the local chemical environment—likely due to the influence of the CDs. These shifts suggest modifications in bonding and charge distribution that can ultimately affect the composite’s overall properties.

Fig. 3 a displays the excitation spectrum for LSAO:5Sm3+, monitored at an emission wavelength of 598 nm. This spectrum is essentially divided into two distinct regions. The first region exhibits a broad absorption band peaking at 290 nm, attributed to the charge transfer band (CTB) from O2- to Sm3+ [48]. In addition, several discrete excitation peaks at 346, 363, 376, 403, 420, 440, 463, and 471 nm correspond to various 4f–4f transitions in Sm3+, specifically from the 6H5/2 ground state to the 4H9/2, 4D3/2, 4D1/2, 4F7/2, 4M19/2, 4I15/2, 4I13/2 and 4I11/2 excited states, respectively—with the 403 nm peak being the most prominent [49]. The presence of multiple excitation peaks in the near-UV region indicates that the LSAO:Sm3+ phosphor is well-suited for wLED applications that utilize n-UV light as an excitation source. Fig. 3b presents the emission spectrum of LSAO:5Sm3+ under 403 nm excitation. This wavelength, which aligns with one of the strongest excitation peaks, yields emission peaks at 561, 598, 644, and 704 nm. These are attributed to the transitions 4G5/2 → 6H5/2, 4G5/2 → 6H7/2, 4G5/2 → 6H9/2 and 4G5/2 → 6H11/2, respectively. Fig. 3c shows the variation of PL intensity with increasing Sm3+ concentration in LSAO:xSm3+ phosphors (x = 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 mol %) under 403 nm excitation. As the concentration of Sm3+ increases, the PL intensity initially rises, reaches a maximum at 5 mol %, and then decreases due to concentration quenching. This quenching occurs because higher dopant concentrations reduce the average distance between Sm3+ ions, thereby enhancing non-radiative energy transfer through mechanisms such as cross-relaxation and multi-phonon relaxation via dipole–dipole, dipole–quadrupole, and quadrupole–quadrupole interactions [50]. Importantly, the emission peak positions remain virtually unchanged with varying dopant levels, and the inset of Fig. 3c, which plots normalized intensity versus Sm3+ concentration, further highlights the material’s suitability for UV–Vis LED applications.

The strongest emission peak at 598 nm arises mainly from a combination of magnetic dipole (MD) and electric dipole (ED) transitions. In contrast, the subsidiary peaks at 561 nm and 644 nm are primarily associated with MD and ED transitions, respectively. The observation that the MD transitions (notably 4G5/2 → 6H5/2 and partially 4G5/2 → 6H7/2) are more intense than the ED transition (4G5/2 → 6H9/2) suggests that the Sm3+ ions reside in a centrosymmetric environment within the LSAO lattice. The efficiency of energy transfer between Sm3+ ions is further analyzed by calculating the critical distance (Rc) using the equation [51]:
(14)

= 2

Here, V is the unit cell volume, χc ​ is the critical concentration for quenching, and N is the number of Sm3+ ions per unit cell. The computed Rc of 18.57 Å indicates that multipolar interactions dominate over exchange interactions in this system. According to Dexter theory [52], the quenching process can be further quantified using:

Here, C is a constant, x represents the Sm3+ concentration, and θ is the multipolar interaction index. With typical values of approximately 6 for dipole–dipole, 8 for dipole–quadrupole, and 10 for quadrupole–quadrupole interactions, our calculated θ value of 6.03 (derived from the slope of the log(I/x) versus log x plot in Fig. 3d) confirms that dipole–dipole interactions are the primary mechanism behind energy transfer and quenching in these phosphors. This efficient energy migration within the host matrix minimizes non-radiative losses and enhances overall luminescence at optimal dopant concentrations.

Fig. 3e demonstrates the strong excitation capability of the CDs, particularly at longer UV wavelengths around 340 nm. The maximum absorption at 340 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition of the C=O band and the π-π* transition of the conjugated C=C bond, reflecting a distinct surface state in the CDs. Two primary factors variation in particle size and the distribution of surface energy traps are proposed to account for the PL behavior of CDs with emission centered around 415 nm. These factors lead to fluorescence through transitions in the conjugated π domain and emission from surface defects. The presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, enhances the aqueous solubility of CDs, offering an advantage over graphene quantum dots, which are typically less soluble [53]. Fig. 3f compares the excitation spectra of CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ with that of LSAO:5Sm3+, monitored at 598 nm. While the LSAO:5Sm3+ spectrum shows several sharp peaks typical of Sm3+ f–f transitions, the composite spectrum displays an additional broad and intense band between 250 and 350 nm, attributable to the π-π* and n-π* transitions in the CDs. This clearly demonstrates energy transfer from the CDs to the Sm3+ ions. Furthermore, the composite exhibits a stronger excitation peak than the bare LSAO:5Sm3+, and it is evident that a n-UV wavelength of 403 nm can simultaneously excite both CDs and LSAO:5Sm3+. Under 403 nm excitation, Fig. 3h also shows the PL spectra of both CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs and bare LSAO:5Sm3+ NPs. The narrow emission band from 320 to 550 nm is attributed to the CDs, which demonstrated a minor peak, demonstrating effective energy transfer from CDs to Sm3+, while the dominant emission peaks at 561, 598, 644 and 704 nm correspond to the Sm3+ transitions (4G5/2 → 6H5/2, 4G5/2 → 6H7/2, 4G5/2 → 6H9/2 and 4G5/2 → 6H11/2, respectively). Notably, the PL intensity of the composite is enhanced by 82.39-fold, which is attributed to efficient FRET from the CDs (donor) to the Sm3+ ions (acceptor). This is further confirmed by the overlap between the emission band of the CDs (320–550 nm) and the absorption peaks of Sm3+ (346, 363, 376, 403, 420, 440, 463, and 471 nm) as shown in Fig. 3g.

The enhancement in emission intensity for composites with different CDs loadings was quantified as 20.35-, 43.87-, 82.39-, 60.55-, and 48.13-fold for CDs loadings of 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 wt%, respectively (Fig. 3i), with 6 wt% being the optimal concentration. The PL enhancement data for various composites are summarized in Table S1. For the CDs(6 wt %)@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs, the significant enhancement in PL intensity is primarily due to FRET, which requires (i) donor–acceptor proximity (1–10 nm), as confirmed by FE-SEM and TEM images showing CDs adhered to the LSAO:5Sm3+ NP surface; (ii) spectral overlap, as evidenced by the overlap in the emission and absorption spectra of CDs and Sm3+ (Fig. 3g) and (iii) favourable dipole orientation between the donor and acceptor. Fig. S5e schematically illustrates the luminescence and energy transfer processes between the CDs and LSAO:5Sm3+. Initially, the CDs absorb UV light, promoting electrons from the C π to the C π* energy levels. These electrons then relax to the lowest LUMO level via transitions such as C π*–N π* and N π*–O π*. As the electrons return to the C π level, blue emission is generated and the energy is transferred to Sm3+ ions, exciting them from the 6H5/2 to the 4F7/2 state. Subsequent non-radiative relaxations lead the electrons to accumulate in the 4G5/2 level, from which they undergo radiative transitions to 6H5/2 (561 nm), 6H7/2 (598 nm), 6H9/2 (644 nm) and 6H11/2 (704 nm) levels. Moreover, some electrons excited to higher energy levels in Sm3+ are recaptured by the CDs and redirected back to the 4G5/2 level via FRET, thereby reducing non-radiative losses and enhancing overall luminescence.

The CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram (Fig. S5a) was used to analyze the PL emission spectra (excited at 403 nm) of LSAO:xSm3+ (x = 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 mol %) and CDs(x)@LSAO:5Sm3+ (x = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 wt %) NCs. The calculated CIE coordinates reveal that all LSAO:xSm3+ samples exhibit orange–red emission, while the CDs(x)@LSAO:5Sm3+ composites display a noticeably brighter orange–red hue. The equal illuminant point is defined as (xi,yi)=(0.333,0.333), which is used to determine the dominant wavelength by drawing a horizontal line from the white point to the reference on the CIE plot. Using this method, the optimized NPs exhibited a color purity of 98.9 %, and the optimized NCs reached a color purity of 99.8 %.

For the CDs(6 wt%)@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs, the color coordinates were determined to be x=0.658 and y=0.341 with the chromaticity epicenter at (xe, ye)=(0.332,0.186), as shown in the CCT diagram (Fig. S5b). The tabulated data in Table 5 includes the color purity, CCT values and corresponding CIE coordinates for all the samples. Fig. S5(c) & S5(d) illustrate the variation in color purity as a function of Sm3+ concentration for the NPs and of CDs concentration for the NCs. All samples exhibit a warm appearance with CCT values below 4000 K, confirming that CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ is a promising luminescent material for warm orange–red LED applications, demonstrating both excellent color purity and favourable CCT values.

The stability of the synthesized phosphor is thoroughly evaluated under various environmental stress conditions, and the results are comprehensively presented in Fig. S6. The stability of CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ composite phosphor was first evaluated under ambient storage conditions. As shown in the PL spectra (Fig. S6a), the emission intensity showed a minimal decrease over a period of 60 days, with the spectral shape remaining consistent. The relative intensity (Fig. S6b) retained approximately 98.93 % of its initial value by day 60, suggesting good luminescence stability during storage. Visual inspection (Fig. S6c) of the powder confirmed its stability, as the orange-red color remained vibrant without signs of degradation or aggregation. To simulate humidity-induced degradation, the phosphor samples are stored in controlled humidity chambers set at 30, 60 and 85 % relative humidity (RH), maintained using saturated salt solutions in sealed containers. For water stability testing, the phosphor powder is first dissolved in water, then filtered, and the residue is dried and collected for further analysis. All samples are observed over a period of several days to assess any optical or physical degradation. The PL spectra shown in Fig. S6(d, g & j) reveal that the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ phosphor experienced only a minor decline in PL intensity, retaining a significant portion of its original emission even after prolonged exposure to high humidity conditions, demonstrating excellent moisture stability. In Fig. S6e, the relative intensity plot demonstrates minimal change from day 1 to day 15, with the phosphor maintaining approximately 97.91 % of its initial emission intensity at 30 % RH. Fig. S6h, showing results at 60 % RH, reveals a slight decrease, with about 96.37 % of the intensity retained. Under high humidity conditions (85 % RH, Fig. S6k), the emission intensity decreases moderately, with the phosphor still preserving around 95.57 % of its original brightness [55]. Visual evidence of these results is provided in Fig. S6f, i & l, where the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs samples stored at 30 %, 60 %, and 85 % RH are displayed under UV light. The physical appearance of the phosphor after humidity exposure shows no visible signs of degradation, discoloration, or clumping across all RH levels. These findings confirm the phosphor's structural stability even in high-moisture environments [56,57].

Photostability under continuous UV irradiation (365 nm) was tested to simulate long-term light exposure. As shown in Fig. S6(m–o), the PL spectra retained their shape with a slight decrease in intensity. The material maintained ∼97.63 % of its original intensity after 120 min of exposure, indicating good resistance to photo-degradation. Visually, the powder retained its orange-red color, further supporting the composite's durability under UV exposure, making it suitable for photonic applications where prolonged light exposure is expected. To further evaluate the chemical durability, the phosphor was subjected to acidic (HCl) and basic (NaOH) solutions, as depicted in Fig. S6p & s. Following 90 min of exposure, the relative intensity remained at 94.47 % and 95.19 %, respectively (Fig. S6q & t), demonstrating that the phosphor maintains its luminescent stability even under harsh chemical conditions. The visual images of the phosphor treated with acidic and basic solutions are shown in Fig. S6r & u. As observed, no visible discoloration occurred after 90 min of exposure. These results highlight the excellent environmental stability of CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs, positioning it as a promising candidate for long-term applications in w-LEDs and other optoelectronic devices. To further assess moisture resistance, the optimized CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ phosphor was immersed in deionized water for 25 h, with the resulting PL spectra shown in Fig. S6v. Fig. S6w illustrates the phosphor's remarkable water stability, maintaining 92.02 % of its initial PL intensity. The images of the phosphor submerged in water are shown in Fig. S6x. After immersion, the phosphor continues to emit strong orange-red luminescence, similar to its original state, confirming that its optical properties are well-preserved even after exposure to water. Overall, the excellent environmental and chemical stability of CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ demonstrated by its resistance to long term storage, humidity, UV, acidic and basic conditions, and water immersion highlights its suitability for long-term use in w-LEDs and other optoelectronic devices. These findings suggest that the phosphor can withstand demanding operational conditions without significant luminescence degradation, making it a promising candidate for advanced lighting and display applications.

Thermal stability is critical for phosphors used in LED applications. The PL spectra of both LSAO:5Sm3+ NPs and CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs were recorded over a temperature range from 298 K to 473 K (Fig. 4 a & b). For the LSAO:5Sm3+ NPs, a rapid decrease in luminescence intensity was observed as the temperature increased. In contrast, the composite sample containing CDs exhibited only a modest reduction in PL intensity over the same temperature range. Notably, while the narrow emission band of CDs in the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ sample diminished more rapidly with increasing temperature (due to thermal quenching), the emission peaks related to Sm3+ remained relatively stable because of the shielding provided by their 4f transitions. It is important to note that the shape and positions of the emission peaks remain largely unchanged for both samples. Fig. 4c & d provide 3D color contour maps of the temperature-dependent PL (TDPL) spectra for the LSAO:5Sm3+ NPs and CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs, respectively. At 423 K, the intensity of the dominant 598 nm emission peak retained 89.0 % of its room temperature value for the LSAO:5Sm3+ NPs and 94.3 % for the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs (Fig. 4e). These values compare favourably with those of other phosphors such as Y2MgTiO6:Sm3+ (79.7 % at 423 K) [58] and NaSrGd(MoO4)3:Eu3+ [59] (Fig. 4f). The high thermal stability observed in the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs can be ascribed to several factors: the stable LSAO host lattice, efficient energy transfer from CDs to Sm3+ ions and the inherently shielded 4f–4f transitions of Sm3+ that reduce non-radiative losses. Additionally, the CDs help passivate defects, further minimizing non-radiative pathways and the low phonon energy of the LSAO matrix suppresses multiphonon relaxation, collectively enhancing luminescence retention at elevated temperatures.

The configurational coordinate diagram (Fig. 4g) provides a conceptual illustration of the thermal quenching mechanism. In this diagram, the excited and ground states are represented by blue and pink curves, respectively. Upon excitation at 403 nm, electrons are promoted from the ground state to the excited 4F7/2 state. The majority of these electrons undergo a non-radiative relaxation to the first excited state (4G5/2, path a) before radiatively transitioning to various ground state levels (6HJ/2, where J = 5,7,9,11) and emitting orange–red light. However, at higher temperatures, some electrons overcome the activation energy (Ea​) barrier and enter the CTB instead of returning directly (process b), then transition non-radiatively (process c), causing emission intensity to decrease due to thermal quenching. Phosphors with a low Ea tend to show rapid declines in emission intensity, whereas those with a high Ea exhibit slower thermal quenching and thus enhanced thermal stability. The activation energy can be estimated using the Arrhenius equation [60]:
(19)

)
where IT ​ is the luminescence intensity at temperature T, I0​ is the initial intensity, C is a constant, and KB ​is Boltzmann’s constant (8.62 × 10-5 eV/K). Fig. 4h & i show that a plot of ln((I0/IT)-1) and 1/T yields a good linear fit. The activation energies, determined from the slope, were 0.25 eV for LSAO:5Sm3+ NPs and 0.31 eV for CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs. These values, which exceed those reported for other Sm3+-doped phosphors (Table 6 ) [61–66], confirm the enhanced potential of these materials for white LED applications. Moreover, the broad emission spectrum of CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ (spanning 350–750 nm) effectively extends the coverage of the LED emission range.

The influence of temperature on the CIE chromaticity coordinates was also examined. Fig. 4j illustrates the relationship between temperature and the CIE coordinates for the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ samples, with the corresponding coordinates listed in Table 7 . Over the temperature range from 298 K to 473 K, only minimal shifts in the coordinates were observed (Δx = 0.00354 and Δy = 0.00369), demonstrating excellent chromatic stability (Fig. 4k). The chromaticity shift (ΔS) was calculated using the equation [67]:

The small value of ΔS (0.0459 at 473 K) confirms that the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs maintain excellent color consistency under thermal stress. Fig. 4l displays the variation of color purity with temperature for the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ sample.

To evaluate the potential of these materials for optical temperature sensing, the temperature-dependent behaviour of both LSAO:5Sm3+ NPs and CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs was investigated. Notably, the positions of the Sm3+ emission peaks remain essentially unchanged between 298 K and 473 K (Fig. 4a & b). However, the fluorescence emission intensity ratio (FIR) between two thermally coupled transitions specifically, the 4G5/2 → 6H7/2 transition at 598 nm and the 4G5/2 → 6H9/2 transition at 644 nm varies significantly with temperature. This variation in FIR, which is critical for optical thermometry applications, follows the Boltzmann distribution and can be expressed as [68]:

These values not only quantify the optical temperature measurement capability but also indicate superior thermal stability in the composite material.

Sensitivity is a critical parameter in optical thermometry. The absolute sensitivity (SA​) defined as the rate of change of FIR with temperature and the relative sensitivity (SR​) defined as the relative change in FIR per degree Kelvin are calculated as follows [69]:

Fig. S7c & d show the temperature-dependent sensitivity curves for both LSAO:5Sm3+ and CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ samples. The absolute sensitivity increases with temperature, reaching maximum values of approximately 0.0015 K-1 for LSAO:5Sm3+ and 0.0038 K-1 for the composite at 473 K. In contrast, the relative sensitivity decreases monotonically with increasing temperature, attaining maximum values of 2.87 % K-1 for LSAO:5Sm3+ and 3.97 % K-1 for CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ at 298 K. These relative sensitivity values exceed those reported for other materials (Table 8 ) [70–75]. The repeatability and stability of the optical temperature sensors were confirmed over six heating–cooling cycles, as shown in Fig. S7e & f, indicating excellent stability of the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs for non-contact optical temperature sensing.

Fig. S7g & h illustrate the variation of δT with temperature for both samples. The resolution generally increases with temperature, however, at 298 K, the minimum thermal resolution was 0.21 K for LSAO:5Sm3+ NPs and 0.10 K for CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs. Overall, the outstanding performance in terms of relative sensitivity, absolute sensitivity and temperature resolution underscores the promise of CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs as advanced optical thermometry materials.

To further understand this energy transfer, decay lifetimes are analyzed. Fig. 5(c–e) presents the decay curves for CDs, LSAO:xSm3+ (x = 0.5–9 mol%) and CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs. The decay curve for pure CDs (Fig. 5c) at an emission wavelength of 415 nm fits a single exponential function [83]:

yielding value of 2.13 ns for CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs. Analyzing the luminescence decay curves provides strong evidence of FRET between the donor (CDs) and acceptor (LSAO:5Sm3+) molecules. A significant difference is observed when comparing the lifetimes of CDs with and without the presence of the acceptor [84]. The decay curve of CDs without the acceptor (Fig. 5c) shows a single exponential decay with a lifetime of 8.45 ns, indicating no FRET, as the longer lifetime implies the absence of energy transfer. In contrast, the decay curve for CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+NCs (Fig. 5c) shows a much shorter average lifetime of 2.13 ns, suggesting efficient FRET, with the donor's energy being transferred to the acceptor. FRET efficiency (η) can be calculated using the formula:
(32)
ɳ = 1 −
τ
DA

where τDA ​ is the donor's lifetime in the presence of the acceptor, and τD ​ is the donor’s lifetime alone. Given τD =8.45 ns and τDA =2.13 ns, the FRET efficiency is found to be approximately 74.79 %. This indicates that 74.79 % of the donor's energy is transferred non-radiatively to the acceptor, demonstrating high FRET efficiency, signifying that the donor and acceptor are well within the optimal 1–10 nm range for energy transfer. This substantial reduction in the donor’s fluorescence lifetime confirms that a significant portion of the CDs excited-state energy is efficiently transferred to the Sm3+ ions, validating the FRET process between CDs and LSAO:5Sm3+ NPs. Furthermore, the observed enhancement in luminescence for CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs compared to LSAO:5Sm3+ NPs when excited at 403 nm (Fig. 5d & e) further supports the efficient FRET mechanism occurring between the CDs and the Sm3+ ions within the nanocomposite [85].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs in orange-red LED and cool w-LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the practical utility of the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs, electroluminescence (EL) spectra of fabricated LEDs were examined. Fig. 5f shows the EL spectrum of a bright orange–red LED, which mirrors the emission peaks observed in the PL spectrum of the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs (refer to Fig. 3h). The EL spectrum of bright orange-red LED includes an n-UV band centered around 405 nm, which is the excitation source, along with four intense peaks at 561, 598, 644 and 704 nm that originate from the emission of LSAO:5Sm3+ phosphors.

For the fabrication of a w-LED, the device emits bright white light at a driving current of 350 mA and an applied voltage of 3 V. Fig. 5g displays the EL spectrum of the w-LED, and the device achieves an impressive color rendering index Ra of 92 along with a CCT of 5666 K. These performance metrics are superior to those of many commercial w-LEDs. In the inset of Fig. 5h, images of the packaged orange–red LED and w-LED are shown, both emitting bright light. The CIE chromaticity coordinates for the w-LED (Fig. 5h) were calculated to be (0.329, 0.325), which is very close to the standard white light point of (0.333, 0.333). Furthermore, color rendering performance is assessed by evaluating various color indices (R1 through R14), where R1–R8 contribute to the average color rendering, Ra and R9 represents the saturated red color. As depicted in Fig. 5i, the fabricated w-LED exhibits a higher Ra (92) and R9 (85) compared to a typical commercial w-LED (Ra = 80 and R9 = 15) [86]. These results indicate that the synthesized CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs are highly promising as a phosphor material for LED applications, especially in producing high-quality orange–red and white light.

Human fingers and palms continuously secrete sweat and sebum due to the high density of sweat and sebaceous glands present on their skin. As long as an individual is active, these secretions are naturally discharged, resulting in the deposition of fingerprint impressions when fingers or palms contact any surface. These impressions are composed of various inorganic salts (e.g., phosphates and chlorides), organic materials (such as fats and amino acids), and water. The inherent adhesive properties of these substances facilitate the binding of the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ powder onto the FP residues. To assess and compare the performance of pure LSAO:5Sm3+ powder with that of the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ composite for LFP detection, FPs were developed under UV 365 nm illumination. As shown in Fig. 6 a & b, the ridge patterns appear with well-defined streamline lines, and various minutiae such as the right loop, dot, cross-over, enclosure, ridge end, hook, bifurcation, double bifurcation, short ridge, island, eye, scar, crease, and sweat pores are distinctly resolved. FP classification is often stratified into levels: the level 1 features represent the global ridge flow and singularities (for example, the right loop), level 2 features comprise local minutiae such as dots, cross-overs, enclosures, ridge endings, hooks, bifurcations, double bifurcations, short ridges, islands, and eyes which are the primary details used in automated matching [87] and level 3 features include finer details like scars, sweat pores, and creases. Among these, sweat pores are particularly valuable for personal identification, given their high stability over time [88]. When comparing the two powders, the FP image developed with pure LSAO:5Sm3+ (Fig. 6a) appears less distinct, whereas the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ sample (Fig. 6b) shows significantly enhanced ridge definition and improved visibility of minutiae. This improvement is ascribed to the enhanced fluorescent characteristics provided by the CDs, which yield superior contrast and clarity upon encapsulation. These findings indicate that the integration of CDs into the LSAO:5Sm3+ matrix markedly improves LFP detection, thereby bolstering its utility in forensic investigations. Enhanced luminescence facilitates the precise delineation of FP minutiae, which is crucial for criminal analysis and biometric security systems.

To further evaluate the consistency, reproducibility and overall efficacy of the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ powder for visualizing LFPs across different individuals, FPs from various donors were analyzed. Fig. 7 a presents LFPs from several donors, underscoring the natural variations in FP characteristics such as ridge density, sweat secretion and minutiae distribution. These variations can affect the quality and clarity of the developed FPs. By comparing multiple donor samples, the robustness of the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ powder was confirmed through gray value plots (Fig. 7b) that display luminescent intensity variations across ridges and furrows, with peaks representing ridges and troughs indicating furrows. This consistency reinforces the reliability and applicability of the method in forensic contexts, where FPs from different individuals must be accurately visualized and analyzed.

To broaden the scope of application, LFPs were also developed on a range of substrates, including coins, leaves, plastic bottles, metal rulers, marble, and paper. Fig. 7c compares bare FP images under visible light with luminescent FP images obtained using CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs under a 395 nm UV lamp on various surfaces. In all cases, the developed FP patterns maintained high quality, and the fluorescent emission was distinct from the background, yielding well-defined ridge flows and overall pattern configurations. Fig. 7d further corroborates these findings through 3D interactive surface plots that reveal detailed ridge topography and fluorescence intensity variations [89], confirming that the phosphor exhibits excellent precision and contrast enhancement for LFP detection.

A series of experiments were conducted to examine the influence of external conditions on the quality of the developed FPs. As water evaporates and lipid secretions degrade over time, the finer features of a FP may become obscured, posing challenges for identification in older prints. In this study, CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs were used to visualize FPs on aluminum foil, with samples aged from 1 day to 210 days (Fig. S8a & b). Notably, even after 210 days, the fluorescence image showed almost no discernible changes, as confirmed by the surface profile plot in Fig. S8c. Additionally, the photo-stability of the developed FPs on a glass surface was assessed under continuous UV 365 nm illumination for up to 240 min (Fig. S8d & e). The images reveal that well-defined ridge patterns with level I–III features remain intact, and this stability is further supported by the corresponding profile plot in Fig. S7f. Thermal stability was also tested by subjecting a FP on marble to elevated temperatures. Fig. S8g & h illustrate that even when heated up to 300 °C, the FP retained prominent ridge features, as evidenced by the surface profile plot in Fig. S8i.

For a more detailed forensic analysis, left and right thumb impressions from a 25-year-old female donor were collected and visualized using CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs. The analysis focused on level 3 features, commonly referred to as poroscopy, which involves the examination of sweat pores and other fine details. These minute features are particularly important in forensic investigations yet are often difficult to capture with conventional FP powders. Sweat pores, in particular, are highly stable and serve as reliable identifiers since their characteristics exhibit minimal change over time [90]. In the poroscopy analysis, details such as pore position, type (open or closed), separation, area and shape are meticulously examined. Fig. 8 a & b display poroscopic images of the right and left thumbs, respectively, where pores are observed at various positions such as inter-ridge locations, near the center, or close to the ridge periphery. The analysis shows that the right thumb generally exhibits smaller pore areas compared to the left, though the pore separation remains largely similar between the two. Additionally, while the right thumb FPs shows pores of various shapes (oval, square, pentagon, and circle) as seen in Fig. 8c, the left thumb predominantly displays oval, rectangular, circular and triangular pores (Fig. 8d). The observed poroscopic differences between the two thumbs are summarized in Table S2.

Further quantitative analysis involves ridge counting. Instead of limiting the count to the area between singular points, the entire fingertip is considered. The core point, which marks the turning point of the innermost ridge, is crucial in biometric analysis and FP scanning, serving roles in indexing, classification, alignment, orientation field modeling and identification. This region is characterized by high ridge curvature and consistent bidirectional ridge flow. For the purpose of this study, ridge counts on the upper left (denoted as ‘a’) and upper right (denoted as ‘b’) regions are taken along predefined lines (yellow and blue, respectively), and the total ridge count is computed as the average:
(33)
R . C =
1 2
(
a + b
)

The resulting ridge counts, documented in Table S3, reveal that the left thumb LFP has a higher ridge count compared to the right. In addition, the thumb tip area was measured using ImageJ software [91], yielding 512.89 mm² for the right thumb and 702.85 mm² for the left. These results align with recent studies that report variations in ridge density and count [92,93], with the coarser nature of the right thumb ridges leading to a lower ridge count relative to the left thumb. This difference approximately is attributed to the inherently finer epidermal ridges typically observed on the left thumb. Fig. 8i & j provide 3D interactive plots of a selected ridge containing a high density of sweat pores, clearly demonstrating that the right FP tends to have coarser ridges. Further, the 3D interactive plots of closed and open pores in Fig. 8 e, f, g & h highlight the remarkable developing selectivity of the NCs. Under 365 nm excitation, the CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs preferentially adhere to the edges of the sweat pores rather than filling them, resulting in bright white outlines against darker pore regions. This pronounced contrast between raised and recessed areas allows for clear differentiation between open and closed pores. Typically, quantitative height (or depth) information is used to analyze material deposition on lipid surfaces [94], underscoring the precision of LFP development achieved with these NCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Artificial intelligence MATLAB processed LFP detection and comparison
ce:section.ce:para: The integration of MATLAB a powerful tool in digital image processing and artificial intelligence with LFP analysis has emerged as an effective approach for detailed forensic examinations. This method employs sophisticated computer algorithms to extract and analyze local features from FP images. For reliable recognition, an LFP image must exhibit clear primary (global) and secondary (local) features. The MATLAB based procedure begins by converting the original color FP image into a grayscale image. This conversion is followed by noise reduction steps, where contaminants such as fur, dust and grains are removed and the image brightness is normalized to enhance the visibility of FP contours and minutiae. The processed image is then binarized assigning pixel values of 0 (black) or 1 (white) based on grayscale intensity and subsequently thinned so that the ridge lines are reduced to a single pixel in width [96]. This series of steps (illustrated in Scheme 2 ) enables the successful extraction of Level 1 and Level 2 FP features, such as the core, islands, bifurcations and so on.

Once the FP image has been processed, it is input into the MATLAB program for further analysis. For comparative purposes, clearly defined regions containing ridges and valleys are designated as target areas while the remaining areas serve as the background. Initially, the original twin FP images (Fig. 12 a) are converted to grayscale (Fig. 12b). Next, segmentation is applied to isolate the FP region from the background, thereby minimizing noise interference. A median filter [97] is then used to further reduce any residual noise. Through threshold-based binarization (using a specified threshold T), the image is divided into target areas (assigned a value of 0) and background areas (assigned a value of 1), as seen in Fig. 12c. Thinning the binary image (Fig. 12d) refines the ridge structures, and by removing extraneous endpoints along the edges and extracting minutiae points, a characteristic FP feature image is obtained (Fig. 12e). This final feature map is then matched against a database to determine the degree of similarity.

For quantitative analysis, the coordinates of the extracted minutiae points are organized into an m × 2 matrix. In Eq. (30), matrix Z represents the sample FP and matrix X represents a control FP [98]:

Here, (zi1,zi2) and (xi1,xi2) represent the coordinates of the feature points, and (a, b) is the coordinate of the core point. A higher similarity score P indicates a greater resemblance between two FPs. In our system, a similarity score greater than 85 % implies that the FPs belong to the same individual, whereas twin FPs typically achieve scores above 70 % but below 85 %, reflecting their intrinsic uniqueness. For instance, the MATLAB program computed similarity scores of 72.61 % for male twin FPs, 75.97 % for male–female twin FPs and 76.89 % for female twin FPs (Fig. 12f). Additionally, individual FPs from a single donor on different substrates such as coins, leaves, plastic bottles, metal rulers, marble and paper yielded similarity scores exceeding 85 % (85.32 %, 85.55 %, 88.67 %, 92.10 %, 91.39 % and 90.82 %, respectively; see Fig. S9d), confirming their identity. The MATLAB code used for these computations is provided in Fig. S9(a–c).

In summary, the successful integration of CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs with a MATLAB-based image processing facilitates rapid and accurate LFP imaging and recognition. This intelligent system significantly advances traditional FP recognition methods and holds great promise for practical forensic applications.

Herein, a novel luminescent NCs CDs@LSAO:Sm3+ was successfully synthesized by embedding blue-emitting CDs into LSAO:Sm3+ nanophosphors. Notably, the PL intensity of the NCs exhibited an 82.39-fold enhancement relative to the pure LSAO:Sm3+ phosphors, a result attributed to a FRET mechanism between the CDs and Sm3+ ions. Furthermore, the NCs achieved exceptional color purity (99.8 %) and an IQE of 87.49 %, highlighting their strong potential for high-performance solid-state lighting applications such as orange–red LEDs and white LEDs. Beyond lighting, temperature-dependent PL studies demonstrated that the NCs possess outstanding thermal stability, retaining 94.3 % of their initial luminescence at 423 K and exhibiting an activation energy of 0.31 eV. Optical thermometry measurements further revealed a high relative sensitivity of 3.97 % K-1 at 298 K, absolute sensitivity of 0.0038 K-1 at 473 K, and a low temperature resolution of 0.10 K. In addition, the strong and stable luminescence properties of the NCs were exploited for forensic applications. They enabled clear visualization of LFP features across all three levels including poroscopic details of left and right thumb impressions and facilitated detailed twin FP analysis. In studies involving male twins, male–female twins and female twins, the NCs enhanced the detection of critical minutiae features, thereby underscoring both the inherited similarities and the subtle individual differences necessary for forensic differentiation. Furthermore, an AI-enabled MATLAB algorithm was implemented for automated LFP comparison, yielding similarity scores that confirmed twin FPs shared over 70 % similarity, while prints from the same individual consistently surpassed 85 %. This high accuracy and reproducibility emphasize the practical applicability of these NCs in forensic security and biometric authentication. Overall, the optimized CDs@LSAO:5Sm3+ NCs exhibit exceptional photophysical properties, robust thermal stability, and versatile applications across solid-state lighting, optical thermometry and advanced forensic identification. Future work will focus on optimizing the synthesis process for large-scale production and further enhancing stability under real-world conditions, paving the way for next-generation optoelectronic and security technologies.
